# 🛡️ Hybrid Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) on ESP32 Edge Devices

![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)
![Platform: ESP32](https://img.shields.io/badge/Platform-ESP32-orange.svg)
![Framework: ESP-IDF](https://img.shields.io/badge/Framework-ESP--IDF-red.svg)
![Cryptography: X25519 + ML-KEM-512](https://img.shields.io/badge/Cryptography-X25519%20%2B%20ML--KEM--512-brightgreen.svg)
![Protocol: AES-256-GCM](https://img.shields.io/badge/Protocol-AES--256--GCM-blueviolet.svg)

A native, bare-metal implementation of a **Stateful Hybrid Post-Quantum Key Agreement and Bidirectional Secure Telemetry Protocol** running on the Espressif ESP32 microcontroller. 

This repository demonstrates how to mathematically bind classical **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (X25519)** with NIST-standardized lattice-based **Post-Quantum Cryptography (ML-KEM-512)** to secure IoT edge devices against future decryption by Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computers (CRQCs) while preserving compatibility with classical security standards.

---

## 🌟 Overview

The security of modern Internet of Things (IoT) devices relies on public-key cryptosystems like RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) to establish session keys. However, Shor's algorithm running on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer can solve the discrete logarithm problem in polynomial time, compromising these systems and enabling **Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL)** attacks. 

While NIST has standardized post-quantum algorithms like ML-KEM (Kyber), migrating immediately to unproven algorithms poses cryptographic risks. This project implements a **Hybrid Paradigm** to address this transition phase:

$$\text{session\_key} = \text{HKDF-SHA256}(\text{X25519\_shared\_secret} \mathbin{\Vert} \text{ML-KEM\_shared\_secret})$$

The resulting session key is cryptographically bound. The communication channel remains secure as long as **at least one** of the constituent algorithms remains computationally secure.

---

## 🏗️ System Architecture & Protocol Flow

```
ESP32 Edge Client                                       Python Server
       |                                                      |
       |--------------- 1. POST /handshake ------------------>|
       |  [Mode=2][X25519_pk (32B)][MLKEM_pk (800B)]          |
       |                                                      |
       |                                                      |-- Perform X25519 ECDH
       |                                                      |-- Perform ML-KEM Encaps
       |                                                      |-- Derive Session Key
       |                                                      |
       |<-------------- 2. Handshake Response ----------------|
       |  [Session_ID (16B)][X25519_pk (32B)][MLKEM_ct (768B)]|
       |                                                      |
       |-- Extract Session_ID                                 |
       |-- Decapsulate ML-KEM Ciphertext                      |
       |-- Compute X25519 Shared Secret                       |
       |-- Derive Session Key                                 |
       |                                                      |
[Loop Every 5s]                                               |
       |                                                      |
       |-- Encrypt Telemetry via AES-GCM                      |
       |                                                      |
       |--------------- 3. POST /api/telemetry -------------->|
       |  [Session_ID (16B)][IV (12B)][CT][Tag (16B)]         |
       |                                                      |
       |                                                      |-- Lookup Key by Session_ID
       |                                                      |-- Decrypt Telemetry
       |                                                      |-- Construct Command Resp
       |                                                      |-- Encrypt Response
       |                                                      |
       |<-------------- 4. Encrypted Response ----------------|
       |  [IV (12B)][Ciphertext][Tag (16B)]                   |
       |                                                      |
       |-- Decrypt Response                                   |
       |-- Execute Commands                                   |
```

### Protocol Phases:
1.  **Stateful Handshake:** The ESP32 generates ephemeral keypairs for X25519 and ML-KEM-512, serializes the public keys, and transmits them to the server.
2.  **Shared Secret Agreement:** The server performs X25519 Diffie-Hellman and encapsulates the post-quantum key, returning a 16-byte `Session ID`, the server's X25519 public key, and the 768-byte ML-KEM ciphertext.
3.  **Hybrid Key Derivation:** Both client and server derive an identical 256-bit session key by concatenating the classical and quantum shared secrets through HKDF-SHA256. All intermediate private key material and shared secrets are immediately zeroized in memory to prevent cold-boot attacks.
4.  **Bidirectional Secure Communication:** Subsequent telemetry posts by the client and command responses from the server are encrypted using hardware-accelerated **AES-256-GCM** with unique nonces (IVs) generated for each packet.

---

## 📚 Research Paper & Literature Review

The accompanying research paper manuscript (`docs/paper_draft.md`) has been significantly expanded to include a comprehensive **IEEE-format literature review** featuring ~40 authentic academic references spanning:
- PQC Foundations & NIST Standardization (FIPS 203)
- PQC on Embedded Microcontrollers (pqm4, RISC-V, ESP32)
- Hybrid Protocol Designs (TLS 1.3, IETF drafts)
- The Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL) Threat Model

It also features a new **Comparative Efficiency Analysis (Table IV)**, directly benchmarking this ESP32 hybrid implementation against other standalone and hardware-accelerated PQC implementations found in current academic literature.

---

## 📊 Benchmarks

An automated 100-iteration benchmarking suite was executed on an ESP32-D0WD-V3 (240 MHz Tensilica Xtensa LX6 CPU, 520 KB SRAM). The empirical results demonstrate the practical viability of hybrid PQC:

| Metric | Classical (X25519) | PQC (ML-KEM-512) | Hybrid (X25519 + ML-KEM) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Total Handshake Latency** | 4.0 ms | 17.3 ms | **21.3 ms** |
| **Peak SRAM Allocation** | 2.1 KB | 11.8 KB | **13.8 KB** |
| **Handshake Payload Size** | 81 Bytes | 1585 Bytes | **1649 Bytes** |
| **Mean CPU Cycles (240MHz)**| 780,000 | 3,950,000 | **4,730,000** |

*Note: The hybrid protocol resource usage scales linearly. It consumes less than 3% of the ESP32's available internal SRAM, preserving remaining resources for main application logic.*

---

## 📦 Protocol Byte Layouts

### Handshake Request (833 Bytes)
```
+---------------+------------------------+---------------------------------------+
|  Mode (1B)    |   X25519 Public (32B)  |         ML-KEM-512 Public (800B)      |
+---------------+------------------------+---------------------------------------+
```

### Handshake Response (816 Bytes)
```
+---------------------+------------------------+---------------------------------+
|   Session ID (16B)  |   X25519 Public (32B)  |      ML-KEM-512 Ciphertext (768B)|
+---------------------+------------------------+---------------------------------+
```

### Client Telemetry POST Payload
```
+---------------------+-------------------+------------------------+-------------+
|   Session ID (16B)  |      IV (12B)     |   Ciphertext (Variable)|  Tag (16B)  |
+---------------------+-------------------+------------------------+-------------+
```

---

## 📁 Repository Structure

```text
├── dashboard/               # Real-time dashboard for telemetry and connection health
├── docs/                    # Research documentation and academic drafts
│   ├── paper_draft.md       # Expanded IEEE journal format paper manuscript
│   ├── walkthrough.md       # Detailed technical execution protocol walkthrough
│   ├── manual_demo.md       # Step-by-step instructions to run the system manually
│   └── ieee_latex_prompt.md # High-octane prompt to generate compile-ready LaTeX code
├── firmware/                # ESP32 FreeRTOS (ESP-IDF C Project)
│   ├── components/
│   │   └── mlkem512/        # Reference C-implementation of ML-KEM-512
│   └── main/
│       ├── main.c           # Stateful HTTP client loop and task execution
│       ├── crypto_hybrid.c  # Handshake, KDF, and GCM encryption/decryption
│       └── benchmark.c      # CPU cycle-accurate benchmarking harness
├── server/                  # Python backend server
│   └── server.py            # Async server managing telemetry, sessions, and KDF
└── telemetry_capture.pcap   # Live Wireshark-compatible packet capture file
```

---

## 🚀 Running the System Manually

Refer to [docs/manual_demo.md](docs/manual_demo.md) for full step-by-step instructions.

### 1. Start the Server
```bash
cd server
source venv/bin/activate
python3 server.py
```

### 2. Compile and Flash the ESP32
Ensure the ESP-IDF environmental variables are loaded:
```bash
cd firmware
source ~/esp/esp-idf/export.sh
idf.py build
idf.py -p /dev/ttyUSB0 flash monitor
```

### 3. Verify Packet Security
Use the saved packet capture or record a new one using `tcpdump`:
```bash
wireshark telemetry_capture.pcap &
```

---
